title: TPU v1 · Writing the verification plan: map every feature to stimulus, coverage, and acceptance criteria
oneLine: Functional verification starts with a plan, not a test. Decompose the TPU specification into verifiable features—matrix-multiply correctness, supported precisions and dimensions, accumulator overflow/saturation, deadlock-free handshakes, sequences of the dozen-plus CISC instructions, and clock-domain crossings. For every feature, define the stimulus that exercises it, the coverage that proves it was exercised, and the measurable criterion for completion. A feature absent from this table has no verification owner.

legend.name: Design specification (source of verification)
legend.desc: Collect the complete DUT behavior from architecture/microarchitecture documents and the deliverables from stages ④ and ⑤: the 256×256 INT8 systolic array, 24 MiB software-managed Unified Buffer, 4 MiB 32-bit accumulators, the dozen-plus CISC instructions issued by the host over PCIe, and weight-stationary dataflow. Every “shall” in the specification must become a verifiable feature or it remains a blind spot.

legend.name: Feature inventory (decomposed item by item)
legend.desc: Split the specification into nonoverlapping features with independent pass/fail outcomes: matrix-multiply correctness; every precision and dimension, including full 256 and partial tiles; accumulator overflow/saturation; deadlock-free handshake/backpressure; CISC instruction decode, ordering, and sequences; and CDC between the PCIe and array-core domains. Fine decomposition enables ownership, tracking, and closure.

legend.name: Three-part mapping (stimulus / coverage / criteria)
legend.desc: Every feature must answer three questions: what stimulus exercises it (directed + constrained random + corner cases), what coverage proves it was reached (covergroup / SVA / code coverage), and what quantitative criteria complete it (coverage closure, 0 unresolved bugs, and an all-green regression). Missing any part means the feature is not planned.

legend.name: Stimulus strategy
legend.desc: For arithmetic with a clear golden result, combine directed vectors with large constrained-random sweeps. For accumulator boundaries, target maximum positive/negative, exact overflow, and exact saturation. For handshakes and instructions, exercise legal and illegal sequences, back-to-back traffic, and backpressure.

legend.name: Completion criteria
legend.desc: Turn “verification complete” into signable metrics: functional and code coverage meet target—often 100%, with a documented waiver for every uncovered bin—all assertions pass, the bug curve converges, and the full regression is green on final RTL. Fixing criteria upfront prevents subjective closure.

steps.cap: ① Decompose the specification into independent pass/fail features: matrix-multiply correctness, precisions/dimensions, overflow/saturation, deadlock-free handshakes, instruction sequences, and CDC.
② Define arithmetic stimulus: use directed plus constrained-random testing for correctness, and specifically target maximum, exact-overflow, and saturation corners of the 32-bit accumulator.
③ Set coverage targets for every feature: cover K∈{1,64,128,256} with a covergroup, and use SVA to require bounded req→ack behavior and no deadlock.
④ Cover instruction sequences and CDC: generate legal, illegal, and back-to-back instruction streams; add CDC assertions and metastability injection across clock phase relationships.
⑤ Fix completion criteria: functional + code coverage closure, zero assertion failures, converged bugs, and an all-green regression. Only then is a feature verified.

before: Define the DUT and verification boundary: obtain the DUT specification from stages ④ and ⑤—the 256×256 INT8 array, 24 MiB Unified Buffer, 4 MiB 32-bit accumulators, dozen-plus CISC instruction interface, and weight-stationary dataflow. State what is in scope and what, such as analog I/O or the PCIe PHY, is out.

Build the feature inventory: decompose the specification into nonoverlapping, independently decidable items: matrix-multiply correctness / precisions and dimensions / overflow and saturation / deadlock-free handshakes / instruction sequences / CDC. Every omission creates a verification blind spot.

Define the reference model (golden): prepare a bit-exact high-level matrix-multiply plus fixed-point accumulation model. The scoreboard compares each DUT result against it; this model defines correctness.

Choose stimulus per feature: use directed plus constrained-random testing where a golden answer exists, targeted corners for boundary behavior, and legal/illegal sequences for handshakes and instructions. Require random seeds to be reproducible.

Set completion criteria: quantify closure through functional/code coverage targets, zero assertion violations, bug convergence, and an all-green regression. Write these before implementation as the signoff basis.
during: Rule: no feature without acceptance criteria. Every planned feature must include stimulus + coverage + completion criteria. “Test matrix multiplication” without proof of coverage or a finish condition is not a plan.

Measure coverage; do not count vectors. Create covergroups for key dimensions (illustrative: cp_size: coverpoint mat_k { bins b[]={1,64,128,256}; }, standard example rather than private TPU code). Track functional coverage, not raw test count.

Plan boundary and corner cases explicitly. Do not rely on random stimulus to discover 32-bit accumulator overflow and saturation. Target maximum positive/negative, exact overflow, and exact saturation; cover both a full 256 array and partial tiles under weight-stationary execution.

Use assertions for control behavior, not output inspection alone. Express handshake/backpressure invariants in SVA (illustrative: assert property(@(posedge clk) req |-> ##[1:N] ack);) to catch deadlock and lost transfers. Cover legal, illegal, back-to-back, and out-of-order instruction arrivals.

Treat CDC as an independent feature. For paths between the PCIe host and array-core domains, plan synchronizer structural checks + formal CDC + metastability-injection simulation across clock phase relationships.

Methodology scope: UVM environments, SVA, covergroups, formal analysis, simulation, and emulation are standard verification practices for large accelerators. TPU's private verification environment and metrics are not public; this section uses documented structures only as the DUT context.

result.label: Primary plan deliverable
result.value: Feature → stimulus / coverage / criteria traceability table (verification plan)

result.label: Example feature set
result.value: Matrix multiply · precision/dimensions · overflow/saturation · handshake · instruction sequence · CDC

result.label: Reference model
result.value: Bit-exact matrix multiply + fixed-point accumulation golden model for scoreboard comparison

result.label: Completion criteria
result.value: Functional + code coverage closure · zero assertion failures · all-green regression

after: Review the plan: architecture, design, and verification jointly inspect the feature inventory for omissions and overlaps and confirm that every item has stimulus + coverage + criteria. Build the environment only after approval.
Track coverage convergence: monitor the planned covergroups and code coverage weekly. For every uncovered bin, add stimulus or record a documented waiver until closure.
Converge bugs and regressions: classify defects by feature, rerun regression after every fix, and continue until discovery rate approaches zero and the frozen RTL passes the full suite.
Sign off against criteria: inspect coverage, assertion results, and regression status feature by feature. Release functional verification only when all criteria are satisfied.
Hand off to synthesis and downstream stages: deliver functionally signed-off RTL and coverage reports to logic synthesis, together with CDC constraints required downstream.
source: In-Datacenter Performance Analysis of a TPU, Jouppi et al., ISCA 2017 (the functional-verification planning methodology is standard industry practice, not TPU's publicly disclosed private verification environment)
